slos.rationale: Admission set a required escrow fraction on a thin or low-trust counterparty's contract, but the fraction was only a number stamped on the terms; nothing held it, released it on a clean delivery, or forfeited a slice on a breach, so the escrow had no teeth and a breach was debited only to reputation after the fact. The budget gates that a breach forfeits a bounded slice proportional to the shortfall — between zero and the whole stake, never punitive, with the remainder released; companion budgets gate that an escrow posts the admission-required collateral against the specific contract (the held amount re-deriving from the posture), that a fulfilled delivery releases the whole stake, that the disposition recomputes from the bytes (a tampered forfeiture caught even after re-sealing) with every transition on the hash-chained audit log, and that it folds into the existing settlement path (resolved against the same record verdict, capped below the whole stake on demand) — so posted collateral is a mechanical, reconstructable artifact backing the conservative terms a thin standing is admitted on, never a hosted escrow custodian or a payment rail.

slos.statement: A counterparty backs many concurrent contracts with one posted stake, not separate collateral per deal: a content-bound collateral pool binds a single posted stake to the set of contracts it backs and allocates each a per-contract share deterministically — proportional to its admission-required collateral — so what backs each deal re-derives from the bytes; settling a contract draws a bounded, pinpointed forfeiture from the shared stake on a breach (the same proportional slice the settlement verdict measures) and releases the rest of its requirement back to the available balance on a clean delivery, so a clean delivery frees capital for the next contract and a breach is covered from the shared stake; a pool committed below the collateral its open contracts require surfaces a bounded, pinpointed top-up obligation rather than silently over-committing; and every post, draw, release, and top-up recomputes from the bytes alone (the allocations re-derive and the balance reconciles, a tampered allocation caught even after re-sealing) and lands on the hash-chained audit log.
slos.rationale: An escrow backed one contract with collateral held against its delivery, but a counterparty running many concurrent contracts had to lock separate collateral per deal even though its breaches and clean deliveries net out — capital stranded contract-by-contract the way bilateral settlements were stranded book-by-book before netting folded them. The budget gates that settling a backed contract draws a bounded slice proportional to the measured shortfall from the shared stake on a breach and frees the rest back to the available balance on a clean delivery (capital reused, not stranded); companion budgets gate that the pool allocates each contract a share proportional to its admission-required collateral (the collateral analogue of a NettingSet's net positions), that a pool committed below its open contracts' requirement surfaces a bounded top-up obligation that a top-up clears, that every transition recomputes from the bytes (a tampered allocation caught even after re-sealing) with each post / draw / top-up on the hash-chained audit log, and that the pool is content-bound and order-independent — so a counterparty's margin account is a mechanical, reconstructable artifact over the existing escrow / admission / settlement machinery, never a hosted clearing house, a margin custodian, or a payment rail.

slos.statement: A posted stake is not committed beyond what it holds across the pools that draw on it: a collateral ledger folds a counterparty's pools into one view, reconciles what they collectively pledge against the capital it actually holds, and surfaces the same capital pledged across more than one pool as a bounded, pinpointed re-use breach rather than silently over-stating coverage; when a stake backs deals for more than one beneficiary the guard bounds each beneficiary's claim to its deterministic pari-passu share of the held capital, so a forfeiture cannot pay one beneficiary out of capital another has first claim on; and the ledger reads only the existing signed, content-bound pools (a tampered one is refused at fold time), re-derives the re-use bound and the beneficiary apportionment from the bytes alone (a tampered figure caught even after re-sealing), and lands the guard on the hash-chained audit log.
slos.rationale: A collateral pool let a counterparty back many contracts with one posted stake, but a pool only ever re-allocates capital within itself — nothing bounded a counterparty that pledged the same stake across more than one pool, double-counting what actually backs each deal the way a settlement record was double-counted before netting deduplicated it. The budget gates that the ledger pinpoints the same capital pledged twice (a re-pledged contract) as a bounded re-use breach and reconciles the total pledged against the held capital; companion budgets gate that each beneficiary's claim is bounded to its deterministic pari-passu share so a scarce stake is apportioned by priority rather than over-promised, that a tampered pool is refused at fold time while a re-use breach lands on the audit chain and the bound re-derives from the bytes even after re-sealing, and that two folders reading the same pools compute the same co-signable ledger hash — so a rehypothecation guard is a mechanical, reconstructable re-use bound over the collateral the fabric already pools, never a hosted custodian or a trusted third party.

slos.statement: The capital the rehypothecation guard bounds pledges against is evidence-backed, not asserted: a custodian (or the poster's own signed reserve record) issues a content-bound CustodyAttestation over the reserves actually held — itemized into reserve lines whose total re-derives on every verify — and guard_collateral(custody=) reads its proven total as the held figure instead of the asserted default; when the proven reserves fall below what the pools pledge the shortfall surfaces as a bounded, pinpointed under-reserved breach rather than passing on an inflated holdings claim; and the attestation reads only signed, content-bound artifacts, verifies from the bytes alone (a tampered reserve figure caught even after re-sealing, a forged custodian refused with the verifier, an attestation for a different poster refused), and folds into the existing guard and audit path.
slos.rationale: The rehypothecation guard bounded a counterparty's pledges against the capital it held, but that holdings figure was the one input the guard trusted — asserted, not proven — so a counterparty over-stating its real reserves still passed, the way a self-asserted reputation score passed before attestation made standing verifiable. The budget gates that a signed proof-of-reserves is read as the held figure (the guard bounding pledges against proven capital rather than a self-reported number); companion budgets gate that a proven shortfall surfaces as a bounded, pinpointed under-reserved breach, and that the proof is auditable and offline — a tampered reserve figure is refused (the total re-deriving from the line items even after re-sealing), a forged custodian is refused with the verifier, an attestation for a different poster is refused, the under-reserved breach re-derives from the bytes even after re-sealing, and the guard lands on the hash-chained audit log — so the held capital is a mechanical, reconstructable, evidence-backed figure, never a custodian's say-so, a hosted proof-of-reserves auditor, or a trusted third party.

slos.statement: The capital the rehypothecation guard bounds pledges against is bounded by the counterparty's whole obligation set, not one buyer's view: a counterparty (or its auditor) issues a content-bound LiabilityAttestation over the total obligations it owes — itemized into liability lines whose total re-derives on every verify — and prove_solvency folds it against a proven CustodyAttestation into a bounded, offline-verifiable solvency margin (reserves − liabilities); when the proven liabilities exceed the proven reserves the shortfall surfaces as a bounded, pinpointed insolvency breach, and guard_collateral(solvency=) reads the solvency-adjusted held figure (max(0, reserves − liabilities)) as the held figure, so a pledge is bounded against capital not already owed elsewhere; and both attestations read only signed, content-bound artifacts, verify from the bytes alone (a tampered liability figure caught even after re-sealing, a forged issuer refused with the verifier, a custody / liability pair for different posters refused), and fold into the existing guard and audit path.
slos.rationale: Proof-of-reserves bounds a counterparty's pledges against a proven holdings figure, but reserves are only one side of the ledger — a counterparty solvent against one buyer's pledges may be deeply under-water once every obligation it owes is counted, and could prove the same reserves against many buyers while quietly insolvent across all of them, the canonical gap the proof-of-reserves literature closes next with a proof-of-solvency (reserves ≥ total liabilities). The budget gates that a signed liability attestation folds against the reserve proof into a solvency margin the guard reads as a solvency-adjusted held figure (bounding pledges against capital not already owed elsewhere rather than against the gross reserves); companion budgets gate that a proven insolvency surfaces as a bounded, pinpointed breach with zero free capital behind the pledges, and that the proof is auditable and offline — a tampered liability figure is refused (the total re-deriving from the line items even after re-sealing), a forged issuer is refused with the verifier, a custody / liability pair for different posters is refused, a flipped solvency verdict re-derives from the bytes even after re-sealing, and the proof lands on the hash-chained audit log — so the held capital is a mechanical, reconstructable, solvency-bounded figure, never a one-sided reserve claim, a hosted solvency auditor, or a trusted third party.

slos.statement: The liabilities a proof-of-solvency folds are provably complete, not merely internally consistent: a LiabilityAttestation commits its line items into a Merkle root bound in its signed content hash, each creditor gets an offline-verifiable InclusionProof that its claim is a leaf of that root (so a poster cannot drop a creditor without the omitted party detecting it), and check_completeness folds creditors' own proven claims against the attestation into a signed CompletenessProof that pinpoints every omitted or under-stated obligation as an OmissionBreach and raises the attested figure to a completed total prove_solvency(completeness=) reads instead of the attestor's single number — so the solvency margin is bounded by the obligations creditors can prove, not only the ones the attestor chose to list; and the inclusion and completeness proofs read only signed, content-bound artifacts, verify from the bytes alone (a tampered leaf or a forged root is caught, a dropped omission breach is caught by completed ≥ claimed), and fold into the existing solvency and audit path.
slos.rationale: Proof-of-solvency folds a proven liability total against the proven reserves, but the total is still the attestor's single number: a counterparty could under-state what it owes by quietly omitting a creditor and still attest a sound, re-deriving total over the creditors it did list — the canonical second half of a proof-of-liabilities, where each creditor proves its own claim is included in the attested total so the total is provably complete. The budget gates that an inclusion proof verifies a creditor's claim against the attestation's signed Merkle root and detects an omission (a tampered leaf refused, an unknown creditor refused); a companion budget gates that a completeness check pinpoints an omitted creditor and bounds the solvency margin by the completed liability total (tipping a counterparty that looked solvent on the attestor's figure into a pinpointed insolvency); and a third gates that the proofs are auditable and offline — a tampered leaf and a dropped omission breach are refused (the completed total re-deriving and never sitting below the folded claims), a completeness check for a different attestation is refused, and the check lands on the hash-chained audit log — so the liability side a solvency proof folds is a mechanical, reconstructable, complete figure, never the attestor's say-so, a hosted attestation registry, or a trusted third party.

slos.statement: A counterparty cannot present different liability roots to different creditors: a LiabilityAttestation exposes a signed RootCommitment (the liabilities_root and as_of the attestor signed, without the line items) that creditors compare over the attestation exchange, and check_root_consistency groups a set of held attestations by their (poster, attestor, as_of) key and folds any two conflicting roots a poster signed for one instant into a content-bound, offline-verifiable EquivocationProof — pinning the poster, the two signed roots, and the creditors each was shown — so a counterparty that signs inconsistent totals is caught with non-repudiable evidence rather than merely suspected; and the comparison and the proof read only signed, content-bound artifacts, verify from the bytes alone (a forged conflicting root is refused with the attestor's verifier and excluded from a scan, so it cannot manufacture a false accusation), credit a failure against the equivocating poster on the reputation path, and land on the hash-chained audit log.
slos.rationale: Completeness proves each creditor's claim is included in the attested liabilities, but a counterparty issues its liability attestation per relationship: nothing stops it presenting different liability roots — a smaller total — to different creditors, so each creditor's inclusion proof verifies against the root it was shown while the counterparty equivocates across the set. Completeness catches an omission only when the omitted creditor folds its own claim; equivocation hides the omission by showing each creditor a root on which its own claim is present. The budget gates that creditors comparing the signed roots over the exchange surface two roots a poster signed for one instant (the privacy-preserving commitment leaking no line items) and fold them into a non-repudiable EquivocationProof that re-derives each root from the bytes and pins the poster, the two roots, and the creditors; a companion budget gates that the comparison and the proof are auditable and offline — a forged conflicting root is refused with the attestor's verifier and excluded from a scan (so it cannot found a false accusation), an honest set showing every creditor the same root is consistent, the equivocating poster is dinged on the reputation path, and the check lands on the hash-chained audit log — so a counterparty signing inconsistent totals is caught with non-repudiable evidence, never a hosted transparency log or a trusted third party.

slos.statement: A counterparty cannot quietly drop a past liability in a later snapshot: a LiabilityAttestation carries an optional commitment to the prior snapshot's root (link_to / attest_liabilities(prior=)) bound into its signed hash, so a poster's attestations form a hash-linked sequence each as_of strictly succeeding the last (a back-dated link is caught from the bytes), and check_history_consistency walks a poster's snapshots and folds them into a content-bound, offline-verifiable HistoryConsistencyProof — every creditor's committed obligation must persist into the next snapshot unless a signed, creditor-issued Discharge evidences the release, and any unexplained drop is pinpointed as a MonotonicityBreach — so a debt that silently vanishes between snapshots is caught with non-repudiable evidence rather than merely suspected; and the walk and the proof read only signed, content-bound artifacts, verify from the bytes alone (a forged or out-of-window discharge does not explain a drop, a dropped breach is caught by re-derivation, a tampered or unsigned snapshot is excluded as inadmissible), credit a failure against the breaching poster on the reputation path, and land on the hash-chained audit log.
slos.rationale: Non-equivocation catches a counterparty signing different liability roots for the same instant, but it is deliberately scoped to one as_of: a counterparty can still issue a later snapshot that quietly drops a past obligation — a debt committed at T simply absent from the root it signs at T' — and each snapshot is internally sound, so nothing yet ties one attestation to its predecessor. Equivocation is conflict across creditors; this is consistency across time. The budget gates that a LiabilityAttestation links to its predecessor's root (a hash-linked history a creditor can walk) and that check_history_consistency walks a poster's snapshots and pinpoints a creditor obligation that dropped between snapshots without a backing discharge as a MonotonicityBreach in a proof that re-derives from the bytes; a companion budget gates that the walk and the proof are auditable and offline — a signed, creditor-issued discharge legitimately explains a drop while a forged or out-of-window one does not, a back-dated link is refused, a dropped breach is caught by re-derivation, the breaching poster is dinged on the reputation path, and the check lands on the hash-chained audit log — so a debt that silently vanishes between snapshots is caught with non-repudiable evidence, never a hosted transparency log or a trusted third party.

slos.statement: When a counterparty's proven reserves cannot cover every obligation it owes, the insolvency is resolved into who-gets-what rather than merely flagged: a content-bound SenioritySchedule (build_seniority_schedule) ranks the obligations into signed priority tranches (rank 0 most senior), and resolve_insolvency folds a proven CustodyAttestation against a proven LiabilityAttestation (reusing prove_solvency for every tamper/forgery/wrong-poster refusal) and distributes the proven reserves across the obligations by seniority then pari-passu within a tranche into a content-bound, offline-verifiable InsolvencyResolution — a senior tranche paid in full before any capital reaches a junior one, a partly-funded tranche split proportionally to each claim, pinpointing each creditor's bounded CreditorRecovery and the shortfall it bears; and the schedule and the waterfall read only signed, content-bound artifacts, verify from the bytes alone (an over-stated recovery, a re-ordered tranche, or a junior creditor paid ahead of a senior one is refused, the schedule bound by hash), credit a failure against a poster that could not make its creditors whole on the reputation path, and land on the hash-chained audit log.
slos.rationale: A SolvencyProof surfaces an InsolvencyBreach when proven liabilities exceed proven reserves, and a history walk pinpoints a debt that silently vanished — but when the reserves genuinely cannot cover every obligation, nothing yet says which creditors the available capital pays, and in what order: an insolvency is flagged, not resolved, and every creditor is left to assume it is made whole. The rehypothecation guard already apportions a scarce stake across beneficiaries pari-passu; the liability side needs the same, plus the seniority real obligations carry. The budget gates that a signed SenioritySchedule ranks the obligations into priority tranches and resolve_insolvency distributes the proven reserves across them by seniority then pari-passu within a tranche — a senior tranche paid in full before a junior one, a partly-funded tranche split proportionally — pinpointing each creditor's bounded recovery and the shortfall it bears in a resolution that re-derives the whole waterfall from the bytes; a companion budget gates that the schedule and the waterfall are auditable and offline — with no schedule the whole set is one pari-passu tranche and a solvent counterparty makes every creditor whole, an over-stated recovery or a re-ordered tranche is refused even after re-sealing, a wrong-poster schedule is refused at fold time, the unmade-whole poster is dinged on the reputation path, and the resolution lands on the hash-chained audit log — so an insolvency is resolved into who-gets-what, never a hosted receiver, a bankruptcy court, or a trusted third party.

slos.statement: Before the insolvency waterfall distributes a counterparty's reserves, mutual obligations between the estate and a creditor are closed out so the creditor recovers only its net position: a content-bound SetOffStatement (build_set_off_statement / set_off_from_records) states the obligations running both ways between a poster and one creditor — what the poster owes and what the creditor owes back — collapsed to the poster's bounded net liability (max(0, owed − owing)), signed by both parties (a mutually-agreed close-out), and resolve_insolvency(set_off=) reduces each creditor's proven liability to its net claim before distributing — a creditor in debit recovers nothing, and the estate's distributable claims shrink to the true net exposure; and the statements read only signed, content-bound artifacts (the existing LiabilityAttestation and SettlementRecords), verify from the bytes alone (an over-stated set-off claiming a different gross than the attestation, a one-sided statement only one party signed, or a tampered net is refused), are bound into the resolution by hash so the netted waterfall re-derives every net claim from the recorded gross, and land on the hash-chained audit log.
slos.rationale: The insolvency waterfall distributes a poster's reserves across the creditors it owes, but a creditor is often also a debtor of the same counterparty across a web of contracts, and the waterfall pays it on its gross claim while it still owes the estate the other side — real insolvency law resolves this first with set-off (close-out netting), and the fabric already nets bilateral settlements multilaterally but not the liability side before the waterfall. The budget gates that a mutually-signed SetOffStatement collapses the obligations running both ways to the poster's bounded net liability and resolve_insolvency(set_off=) reduces each creditor to its net claim before distributing — a creditor in debit recovers nothing and the distributable estate shrinks to the true net exposure; a companion budget gates that the close-out is auditable and offline — a netted resolution re-derives from the bytes (an inflated set-off caught even after re-sealing), a one-sided or over-stated set-off is refused, the statement is mutually-signed and derives from the existing LiabilityAttestation and SettlementRecords, the bound statements verify against the resolution, and the statement lands on the hash-chained audit log — so a creditor of an insolvent estate is paid on its true net exposure, never a hosted clearing house, a bankruptcy court, or a trusted third party.

slos.statement: The cross-org settlement & credit fabric composes end-to-end as one system, not a pile of primitives: a single CrossOrgEngagement (app.cross_org_engagement) threads the whole pipeline — negotiate → contract → choreograph delivery → settle → net → prove solvency — behind one governed, audited call-path and seals it into one content-bound, signed EngagementNarrative whose hash chain links every stage, recomputes from the bytes alone, binds each artifact's own content hash and a digest of its bytes, and re-verifies every captured artifact (the settlement records, the netting set, the solvency proof) offline; one continuous signed audit narrative runs from the first offer to the final proof on the same hash-chained log.
slos.rationale: Twenty rungs delivered the cross-org primitives, each signed, content-bound, and offline-verifiable on its own — but nothing presented the fabric as one coherent system or proved it composes end-to-end, so a caller still wired ~40 separate calls by hand with no single artifact attesting the whole. The budget gates that a complete engagement composes and seals into a narrative that verifies offline from the bytes alone (the chain links, the head and content hash recompute, the coordinator's signature checks); companion budgets gate that the lifecycle threads every stage in order (a discovered participant included), that every captured artifact verifies and re-digests against its bound digest, that the engagement and every rung land on one continuous hash-chained audit log that recomputes, and that the facade is purely compositional (every primitive stays usable directly, byte-for-byte the same) — so the fabric is proven a system, never a hosted orchestration service.

slos.statement: A tamper introduced anywhere in a composed cross-org engagement is caught from the bytes alone: re-ordering a stage breaks the hash chain, editing a stage's digest or an underlying artifact's bytes fails the digest check, and a forged signature fails authentication — so the engagement narrative is an end-to-end integrity proof over the whole fabric, not merely a transcript.
slos.rationale: Composing the fabric into one narrative is only meaningful if the narrative is tamper-evident: a transcript that cannot detect an edited stage or a swapped artifact proves nothing. The budget gates that a re-ordered stage, an edited stage digest, an edited underlying artifact, and a forged coordinator signature are each caught by verification; a companion budget gates that the whole engagement re-verifies offline with every artifact re-digested against its bound digest — so the engagement narrative is a mechanical, reconstructable integrity proof over every primitive it threaded, never a trusted-party attestation.

slos.statement: The data & analytics plane composes end-to-end as one system, not a pile of primitives: a single DataEngagement (app.data_engagement) threads the whole pipeline — register → profile → sample → screen → query → analyze → chart → governed metric → cite — behind one governed, audited call-path and seals it into one content-bound, signed DataNarrative whose hash chain links every stage, recomputes from the bytes alone, binds each artifact's own published commitment and a digest of its bytes, and re-verifies every captured artifact offline; one continuous signed audit narrative runs from the raw table to the cited deliverable on the same hash-chained log.
slos.rationale: Seven rungs (4.1–4.7) delivered the data plane's primitives — tabular evidence and the compact encoder, profiling / sampling / fit-in-window and the quality rails, governed text-to-query, the analysis agent, content- and data-bound charts, streaming, and the semantic layer — each grounded, cited, and offline-verifiable on its own, but nothing presented the plane as one coherent system or proved it composes end-to-end. The budget gates that a complete engagement composes and seals into a narrative that verifies offline from the bytes alone (the chain links, the head and content hash recompute, the analyst's signature checks); companion budgets gate that the lifecycle threads every stage in order, that every captured artifact verifies and re-digests against its bound digest, that the engagement and every rung land on one continuous hash-chained audit log that recomputes, and that the facade is purely compositional (every primitive stays usable directly, byte-for-byte the same) — so the plane is proven a system, never a hosted query engine, a managed warehouse, or a notebook service.

slos.statement: Every analytical finding a composed data engagement carries is data-bound: given the live catalog, each captured query, analysis, chart, and governed metric re-executes against the content-hashed source and re-derives the answer and every cited cell from the bytes, so the cited deliverable threaded from the raw table is provably grounded in the source it cites — the analytics analogue of a generated report's per-claim entailment, applied to the whole engagement.
slos.rationale: Composing the plane is only meaningful if the composed narrative carries the data plane's distinguishing guarantee — cell-level provenance — across every stage, not merely a chain of opaque digests. The budget gates that the whole engagement verifies with every artifact data-bound: the query result, the analysis narrative, the chart, and the governed metric each re-execute against the content-hashed source and re-derive every cited cell, and the engagement's data_bound verdict re-runs every binder — so a finding is carried only when it re-derives from the bytes, the verifier being the query plane's offline re-execution, never a model.

slos.statement: A tamper introduced anywhere in a composed data engagement is caught from the bytes alone: re-ordering a stage breaks the hash chain, editing a stage's digest or an underlying artifact's bytes fails the digest check, a tampered source breaks data-binding (the findings no longer re-derive), and a forged signature fails authentication — so the data-engagement narrative is an end-to-end integrity proof over the whole plane, not merely a transcript.
slos.rationale: Composing the plane into one narrative is only meaningful if the narrative is tamper-evident: a transcript that cannot detect an edited stage, a swapped artifact, or a tampered source proves nothing. The budget gates that a re-ordered stage, an edited stage digest, an edited underlying artifact, a tampered source (caught by data-binding), and a forged analyst signature are each caught by verification; a companion budget gates that the whole engagement re-verifies offline with every artifact re-digested against its bound digest and every finding re-derived from the source — so the data-engagement narrative is a mechanical, reconstructable integrity proof over every primitive it threaded, never a trusted-party attestation.

slos.statement: A computer-use agent driving the grounded action plane reaches a verified end state within its action budget: on a deterministic, WebArena/OSWorld-shaped app it perceives the screen as typed, addressable elements, grounds each intent to a stable role+name selector (never a brittle pixel coordinate), acts, and post-verifies the effect, finishing the task within the declared step budget with success decided by an end-state oracle rather than turn-by-turn plausibility.
slos.rationale: Computer-use and provider-hosted tools already shipped as a capability, but as a thin GUI adapter, not a grounded, verified action plane: an agent that acts on brittle coordinates cannot replay, cannot survive a layout shift, and cannot tell whether its action had the intended effect. The action plane binds every action to a stable selector and closes a perceive → ground → act → post-verify loop, so success is a reconstructable end-state the same trajectory metrics and test-time search already score — here the budget gates that the agent reaches the verified goal within its action budget, the success-at-budget bar the agentic leaderboards judge on, held offline.

slos.statement: No destructive computer-use action ever executes without approval: a destructive or out-of-scope action (a deletion, a purchase, a navigation outside the permitted scope) is pre-gated like a write tool and refused unless an approval callback grants it, so an agent that attempts one is blocked rather than merely logged — the gate makes an unapproved destructive action structurally impossible, not discouraged, and a divergent action is rolled back to the prior state.
slos.rationale: An agent that drives a real screen can do real, irreversible harm — delete an account, place an order, leave the permitted scope — so the safety bar is not best-effort: every action runs through the same permission and rail policy a write tool does, a destructive or out-of-scope action is refused unless explicitly approved, and on a post-verify divergence the effect is undone (the computer-use analogue of saga compensation). The budget gates that a reckless policy attempting a destructive action without approval performs zero such actions — the gate blocks it — and companion budgets gate that out-of-scope navigation is refused and a divergent action is rolled back, so the action plane is reversible and accountable by construction, not by audit after the fact.

slos.statement: An agent identity is portable and self-certifying — its DID is derived from its Ed25519 public key, so the verifying key resolves from the identifier alone, offline, with no registry — and its identity document verifies from the bytes. Keys rotate along a signed rotation chain: a signature is validated against the key that was current at signing time, so a rotated-away or revoked key cannot forge new identity history, while signatures it made while current stay valid.
slos.rationale: The platform signed every artifact, but who a key belonged to was an out-of-band key_id string — accountability was only as strong as that assumption. Identity makes the key first-class: the DID is the key (self-certifying, offline-resolvable), the document is content-bound, and rotation is a signed chain rather than a swap, so a compromised or superseded key cannot rewrite history. The budget gates that a fresh identity's DID resolves to its key, its document verifies, its rotation chain checks, an old signature survives a rotation, the new key signs new history, and a rotated key cannot pass as current — identity integrity by construction, not by trust.

slos.statement: A signed delegation mints a bounded grant (a subset of capabilities, a budget cap, an expiry, an audience) from a principal to an agent, composing into a delegation chain that verifies offline — each issuer's key resolves from its DID — where every link only attenuates, never amplifies: capabilities only shrink, the budget only tightens, the expiry only shortens. So a tool call, contract signature, or saga handoff carries provenance of authority, and an over-reaching sub-delegation (or a tampered grant) is refused from the bytes.
slos.rationale: Production multi-agent and multi-org systems delegate authority down a chain (principal to agent to sub-agent); without a structural guarantee, a sub-delegation could quietly grant more than it received, and an injected or compromised agent could escalate. Delegation models authority as negative space and checks attenuation as a structural invariant: the budget gates that a valid chain verifies and permits an in-bounds action, refuses an attenuated-away capability and an over-budget request, refuses an over-reaching sub-delegation that amplifies its parent, and catches a tampered grant offline — so authority flows downward with provenance, never sideways or upward, and the guarantee holds from the bytes alone.

slos.statement: An answer carries a checkable certificate a deterministic verifier confirms independently of the model: a kernel set (arithmetic, units, temporal, schema, constraint satisfaction, citation entailment, and the statistical kernels — trend, correlation, confidence/prediction interval, and forecast — that recompute a stated statistic from the cited cells) emits a content-bound certificate that is verified only when it recomputed a claim and it held, and refuted when a recomputation disagreed — so a wrong answer the relevant kernel can see is refused rather than silently emitted. The certificate re-derives its verdict from the recorded checks and binds it into a content hash, so a verdict flipped to verified after the fact is caught from the bytes alone.
slos.rationale: Judges, oracles, and the governance verifier give probabilistic per-answer signals; for the classes of question where it is possible, correctness can instead be certifiable. Soundness is the whole value: a certificate that can call a wrong answer verified proves nothing. The budget gates that the kernels refute a wrong sum, a dimensional mismatch (5 km = 5000 kg), an off-by-one duration, a violated constraint, and an uncited numeric claim, verify a correct one, and — with the statistical kernels — refute a stated trend / interval / forecast the cited data does not bear out and a series whose values do not match the cells it cites, while verifying a correct one, and that a tampered verdict is caught on re-verification — turning 'the judge thinks it is right' into 'here is a proof you can check', the output-side analogue of the governance verifier's machine-checked invariants, held offline.

slos.statement: A statistical conclusion a data answer draws carries a checkable certificate the way an arithmetic one does, and a causal claim must earn its warrant: the correlation kernel recomputes the Pearson correlation of two cited series and confirms a stated value, but refuses a correlation stated as causation that declares no controls and no randomized design, and refutes a controlled causal claim whose association vanishes once the declared confounder is partialled out (the partial correlation collapses below the confounding threshold) — while a genuine controlled association that survives the controls, and a claim warranted by a declared randomized design, are verified. The verdict re-derives from the recorded checks and the cited cells, so a refuted causal claim refuses to emit and drives the bounded self-correction loop.
slos.rationale: The most common way a data answer misleads is not a wrong arithmetic but a wrong inference — reporting a correlation as a cause without the controls that would license it. A judge or an LLM reviewer catches this only probabilistically; here it is a deterministic refutation. The budget gates that an uncontrolled causal claim over two strongly-correlated series driven by a common confounder is refused, that a claim which names the confounder but cannot show the association survives controlling for it (the partial correlation collapses) is refuted, that a genuine association which survives the control and a randomized-design claim are verified, and that the underlying correlation value is recomputed and confirmed — turning 'this sounds like a causal story' into 'the controls the claim asserts are present and the association survives them, or it does not emit', the causal-inference analogue of the arithmetic kernel's refutation, held offline.

slos.statement: A behavior spec states a temporal-logic property over an agent's tool trajectory (never call a write tool before approval), a runtime monitor checks it step-by-step, and a shield wired into the tool runtime blocks a violating action before it executes — so an unapproved write returns a denied result rather than running, while an approved one proceeds. The shield makes a policy-violating action structurally impossible, not merely logged after the fact.
slos.rationale: The governance verifier proves controls ahead of a run and the rails screen input/output, but neither stops a specific unsafe action mid-trajectory. The shield is the per-step, online counterpart: it observes each proposed action and refuses or repairs a violating one before the effect, the behavioral analogue of the ahead-of-run proof. The budget gates that a shield forbidding an unapproved write blocks the call (a denied result) while letting an approved write through — prevention by construction at the action boundary, held offline against a deterministic tool runtime.

slos.statement: An agent grows its own capability open-endedly: app.cultivate proposes tasks at the frontier of current competence, attempts each with a library-composing test-time search, verifies the result against the task-success oracle, distills a winning trajectory into a verified, content-addressed learned skill, and promotes it only through the same no-regression gate a deploy clears. Capability — the fraction of a held-out frontier set the skill library solves by applying a known skill — is at-least-as-good after a cultivation cycle as before, a skill that stops paying its way is demoted rather than silently kept, and the result re-derives its monotonicity verdict from the recorded per-cycle capabilities so an inflated number is caught from the bytes.
slos.rationale: The closed self-improvement loop, RLVR, and the distillation flywheel make an agent better at known tasks; open-ended capability growth (Voyager / ADAS-shaped) is the apex of that arc, and its risk is unbounded drift — a self-proposing agent that degrades on what it already did to chase a new skill. Monotonicity is the safety property that makes growth reversible rather than runaway: each promotion reuses the same gated, significance-aware no-regression check that protects a prompt or policy promotion, so a skill is added only when held-out capability does not fall, and dead weight is demoted. The budget gates that a full propose → attempt → verify → distill → promote run over a deterministic environment ends at least as capable as it began, composes learned skills, and catches a tampered capability number — turning 'the agent taught itself something' into 'the agent is provably no worse and usually better', held offline.

slos.statement: Every objective an autonomous curriculum proposes is gated before it is ever attempted: its instruction is screened by the programmable rails, and the governance verifier must prove the app's controls (containment, residency, budget, erasure) still hold for the round. An out-of-policy objective — one a safety rail blocks, or any objective when the governance invariants do not hold — is pinpointed and refused, never run, and the curriculum proposal re-derives that refusal from its bytes so a refused objective cannot be relabelled as proposed.
slos.rationale: Autonomy that proposes its own tasks is only safe if it cannot propose its way out of the guardrails. The same rails that screen a user request and the same governance verifier that proves containment ahead of a run gate every self-proposed objective, so the autonomy stays inside the controls the platform already enforces rather than around them. The budget gates that an objective tripping a secrets rail is refused and never attempted, that a failing governance verifier refuses the whole round, and that the proposal's content hash catches a refused objective slipped into the proposed set — the autonomous-growth analogue of the shield's prevention-by-construction, held offline.

slos.statement: An app's fitness for production is argued, not asserted: app.assurance_case assembles the evidence the platform already emits — eval and regression gates, the governance verifier, reasoning certificates, the signed audit chain, identity and delegation provenance, SBOM / SLSA attestations — into a content-bound argument tree whose top claim decomposes into sub-claims, each discharged by a hash-bound piece of that evidence. No claim stands on missing or stale evidence: a leaf whose required evidence is absent, expired past its freshness horizon, or falsified is pinpointed and the case does not hold, while a fully-discharged case does, and the whole case re-derives its verdict from the bytes so a flipped verdict or an edited argument tree is caught offline.
slos.rationale: The platform emits abundant evidence — verifier proofs, certificates, gate verdicts, provenance — but never assembled it into one machine-checkable argument that the system is fit for purpose, the assurance-case discipline (GSN / CAE) the safety and regulatory frontier demands. Soundness is the whole value: an assurance case that calls a system fit on evidence that is missing, stale, or false proves nothing. The budget gates that a fully-evidenced case holds and verifies offline, that a leaf demanding evidence it lacks is pinpointed and the case fails, that a proof past its freshness horizon expires and fails its claim, and that a falsified verdict fails its claim — turning 'we believe it is safe' into 'here is a structured proof you can check from the bytes', the capstone that ties every subsystem into one continuously-verified safety argument, held offline.

slos.statement: An assurance case is a living, CI-gated invariant, not a point-in-time audit: it is re-checked on every change (a model swap, a prompt edit, a dependency bump, a new deployment), and assurance_regression_gate turns a claim that was discharged before but is no longer — its evidence now falsified, stale, or missing — into a build failure, exactly as a no-regression gate blocks a quality regression. A signed incident ties an observed production failure to the sub-claim it falsified and the case learns, demanding fresh evidence before it re-validates.
slos.rationale: An assurance argument that is checked once and filed away rots silently as the system changes around it; the value of a continuous assurance case is that a regression in any piece of its evidence fails the build before the change ships. The gate reuses the same machinery that blocks a quality regression, now over the safety argument itself. The budget gates that re-checking an unchanged case passes, that a claim falsified by a change is caught and fails the gate, that the regressed after-case does not hold, and that a signed incident makes the case demand a remediation proof before it re-validates — closing the loop from a production incident back into a stronger, re-verified safety argument, held offline.

slos.statement: Every supported provider's default model and capability-heuristic families, and every openai_compat preset's headline model, resolve to a non-sparse, priced profile in the shipped catalog.
slos.rationale: The data-driven ModelRegistry is the single source of truth the cost PriceTable, the capability guard, the cost/latency router, the model cascades, and the energy accounting all read from. A current-lineup model that resolves to nothing warns once and then bills $0 and falls back to a guessed capability matrix; gating coverage proves the catalog actually prices the real lineup of every provider Vincio supports rather than silently degrading the cost report.

slos.statement: No GA billable price in the catalog has drifted more than the freshness horizon past its priced_as_of date, evaluated against the catalog's release date rather than the wall clock.
slos.rationale: A half-year-old price would otherwise ship green because the registry version was a hand-bumped string with no enforced horizon. Evaluating freshness against the deterministic release date (never the clock) keeps a frozen bug-fix release stable while a new catalog snapshot that carries a price drifted past the horizon fails the build, forcing a refresh.

slos.statement: No GA billable model of a paid provider silently bills $0: every such model carries a real input price (and an output price for generative models).
slos.rationale: An unpriced billable model makes the cost report quietly wrong — the most expensive way to be inaccurate, because spend looks free. Gating that every GA billable model of a paid provider is priced makes the cost report honest by construction; genuinely free or self-hosted models (local, mock) are exempt, and an arbitrary uncatalogued id still warns once rather than billing $0 silently.

slos.statement: The single-pass feature arena selects byte-identical context to the per-pass derivation: the same evidence, the same exclusions, the same conflicts, and the same budget.
slos.rationale: The arena derives each candidate's lexical features once and threads them through scoring, dedup, conflict, and selection; correctness is the precondition for the speedup, so the gate proves the optimization never changes what is selected. The features are byte-identical to the per-pass derivation, so the selection signature must match with the flag on and off.

slos.statement: Single-pass selection over a large candidate pool selects byte-identical context to the per-pass baseline, with or without NumPy present.
slos.rationale: On a pool large enough to engage the single-pass NumPy reduction, the feature arena and the vectorized scoring path must still select the identical context the per-candidate baseline does — NumPy is an accelerator, never a behavior change, so the selection cannot diverge at scale.

slos.statement: The single-pass feature arena is at least 1.05x faster than the per-pass derivation on a large, mostly-distinct candidate pool (the 10k-scale regime where the bounded global feature cache thrashes).
slos.rationale: A selection-preserving optimization is only worth shipping if the win is real and stays real; a ratio floor makes an erased win fail the build rather than passing silently under a loose latency ceiling. The budget gates 1.08 against the published 1.05 to leave headroom on shared runners while still catching a regression that erases the arena's benefit.

slos.statement: Bounded BM25 top-k selection returns the identical hits a full sort would: the bounded result equals the prefix of the full ranking.
slos.rationale: Bounding the top-k with a partial selection (O(n log k)) instead of a full sort (O(n log n)) must not change the hits returned; the gate proves the bounded path reproduces the full-sort ranking exactly for every k.

slos.statement: Each of the five jobs a newcomer actually has — grounded RAG Q&A, a tool-using agent, structured extraction, an eval, and a multi-step flow — plus the fluent Flow, is expressible through a single vincio.tasks entry point (rag / extractor / tool_agent / evaluation / chat / Flow), where the verbose ContextApp path takes a fistful of string-keyed builder calls.
slos.rationale: The platform is feature-complete but its power was buried: the canonical RAG path alone is six coupled calls where an LCEL chain, a LlamaIndex query engine, a DSPy module, or a Haystack pipeline each cost one line. The budget gates that every job is reachable through one task-shaped constructor returning the right facade (benchmarked head-to-head against those competitors in benchmarks/competitive.py); a companion budget gates the count of entry points — so the front door is a real, minimal top layer, never a partial sugar that still drops to the verbose path for the common case.

slos.statement: Each one-line task lowers to the exact same governed ContextApp.run packet and RunResult as the verbose builder form: given the same inputs, the ad-hoc form (rag, the Flow pipeline, extractor) and the hand-written six-call form produce a byte-identical lowering signature — the same packet spec_hash and the same output, citations, eval scores, and token usage — so there is no behavioral fork.
slos.rationale: A one-line front door is only safe to adopt if it changes nothing about what runs: retrieval, grounding, validation, rails, budgets, tracing, and the audit chain must all apply unchanged. The budget gates that the ad-hoc form and the verbose form compile to a byte-identical lowering signature, proven with the shared vincio.testing.run_signature harness (the same lowering harness the 5.2 single-pass feature arena uses for selection byte-identity); a companion budget gates the count of forms proven identical — so the facade is provably pure sugar over the existing governed path, never a parallel stack.

slos.statement: Every task facade exposes .app, the fully-configured ContextApp, reaching every deep method — nothing is shadowed and nothing is unreachable — so the common case is one expression while the complex case drops to all of the platform's methods through the same object.
slos.rationale: A minimal front door must never become a cage: the simplification is only acceptable if it adds no ceiling. The budget gates that every facade's .app is the full ContextApp and that the deep methods (rails, memory, optimization, governance, agents, the cross-org and data-engagement facades) are all reachable through it; a companion budget gates the count of deep methods checked — so the escape hatch is total, the one-liner a strict superset-in-reach of the verbose path.

slos.statement: The documentation is a connected graph, not a pile of leaf pages: every internal link across all concept, guide, and reference pages resolves — both the file path and, for a Markdown target, the heading anchor — so a reader never hits a dead link and a renamed or moved page cannot silently strand a reference.
slos.rationale: The docs are ~80 leaf pages bound by one hand-ordered index; a substring gate only checked that a subsystem name appeared somewhere in the text, so a broken cross-link shipped undetected. The budget gates that every internal link under docs/ resolves (path + anchor), parsed from the authored pages (code blocks excluded), so the connective tissue the capability map and the Related blocks add is provably intact, not asserted.

slos.statement: Every public app.* verb is bound to the concept that explains it, the guide that applies it, the example that demonstrates it, and the reference that specifies it: the generated capability map places every verb under one of the six capability facades, every verb is documented in api.md, and every concept page reaches a guide, an example, and a reference anchor — so a newly-added verb or an undocumented concept cannot ship.
slos.rationale: Before 5.4 the curated api.md documented only ~119 of the public app.* methods and the data-plane concepts reached neither a guide nor their example. The budget gates that the doc graph (vincio._docmap) places all ~203 public ContextApp methods in the capability map and api.md and that every concept reaches a guide + example + reference anchor; a companion budget gates that every concept is connected, so coverage is mechanical and complete rather than hand-maintained and drifting.

slos.statement: A reader traverses the docs laterally, not by returning to the index: every concept and guide carries a current, single-sourced Related cross-link block, the generated pages (capability map, learning path, api.md app-method index) are current, and no docs page is orphaned — every page is reachable from another page.
slos.rationale: The 16 concept pages had zero inbound links and no page connected laterally to the example that demonstrates it. The budget gates that every concept and guide carries the current generated Related block and that the generated pages are current; a companion budget gates that no docs page is orphaned (every page is linked from another), so the navigation graph stays connected and a regenerated artifact can never drift from what is committed.

slos.statement: The open evaluation plane's provenance tiers are airtight: a Tier-S fabricated fixture can never print a Tier-R or Tier-L label, so a result's tier is the tier its inputs actually justify and a static mechanism check can never masquerade as a live score.
slos.rationale: The project's honesty culture — enforced by hand in the README's 'A note on claims' — is here made structural: the engine computes the tier a run may claim from the dataset's provenance and whether the solver is live, and refuses a lower tier trying to print a higher tier's label. The budget gates that the fabricated-fixture ceiling holds and that a forced Live label is rejected, so the tiering is mechanical, not asserted.

slos.statement: Two runs of the evaluation suite over the same fixtures produce a byte-identical result digest, so a Tier-S/Tier-R score is reproducible on any machine and a diff in a committed report is a real change, never sampling noise.
slos.rationale: A reproducible-tier benchmark is only trustworthy if it is deterministic; the budget gates that two full Tier-S runs produce the same content digest (wall-clock and run id excluded), so an offline benchmark number is a stable, content-addressed artifact rather than a one-off measurement.

slos.statement: Every benchmark in the catalog resolves end to end — a live adapter, a dataset, a primary metric, and a report — so a catalog entry can never be a dangling name that looks runnable but is not.
slos.rationale: A pluggable benchmark catalog is only honest if every entry is actually runnable; the budget gates that each registered spec builds its adapter, loads its Tier-S fixture, and declares a metric, so a half-wired benchmark fails the build instead of shipping as a phantom catalog row.

slos.statement: The evaluation-plane gates are non-vacuous: a mislabeled provenance tier, a wrong metric value, and a perturbed re-run are each provably caught, so a green suite means the honesty checks ran, not that nothing was checked.
slos.rationale: A conformance gate that never fails proves nothing; the budget injects a Tier-S-to-Live relabel, an all-wrong metric, and a tampered run outcome and asserts each is detected, so the tier-integrity, metric-correctness, and determinism guarantees are demonstrated to bite.

slos.statement: The two-level public surface stays consistent: every public subpackage __all__ resolves to a live attribute with no duplicate or malformed entries, the classified surface matches the committed manifest (docs/reference/subpackage-surface.txt), and the gate provably bites on an injected dead symbol, duplicate, and malformed __all__.
slos.rationale: vincio.__all__ is frozen, but each subpackage also declares its own __all__ — and that surface had drifted: a handful of names were exported yet referenced nowhere (dead surface that reads as supported API), and the gap to the top level was real but undeclared, with no guard against a name in an __all__ resolving to nothing. The budget gates that the reconciliation holds, so the interior surface cannot silently re-acquire dead symbols or diverge from the declared manifest — exactly as docs_conformance keeps the docs graph honest and registry coverage keeps the model catalog honest.

slos.statement: No public subpackage __all__ exports a name that resolves to no attribute, lists a name twice, or is itself malformed — there is no dead public surface.
slos.rationale: A name in __all__ that resolves to nothing reads as supported API while doing nothing. The budget gates resolvability tree-wide across every public subpackage, so a renamed or deleted symbol left behind in __all__ fails the build instead of shipping as phantom surface.

slos.statement: The classified two-level surface matches docs/reference/subpackage-surface.txt byte-for-byte, so any addition, removal, or TOP/DUP/SUB reclassification of a subpackage __all__ symbol is a deliberate, reviewed edit.
slos.rationale: The subpackage-only public surface was previously undeclared, so it could grow or shrink without review. The budget freezes it the way public-surface.txt freezes the top level: a regenerated manifest that differs from what is committed fails CI, so the interior surface can only change on purpose.

slos.statement: The surface-consistency gate provably bites: an injected dead symbol, a duplicate entry, and a malformed __all__ are each reported, so a green check means the invariants were actually exercised.
slos.rationale: A gate that never fires is indistinguishable from no gate. The budget gates that the detector reports each synthetic breakage, so the consistency guarantee is backed by a check that demonstrably catches the failure it claims to prevent.

slos.statement: Every error raised on a public entry point derives from VincioError: the ContextApp (app.* verb) surface raises no bare built-in, the classified baseline of accepted public built-in raises matches the committed manifest (docs/reference/error-contract.txt), and the detector provably catches an injected public built-in raise.
slos.rationale: Vincio's contract is that one `except VincioError` catches the whole family and `.code` is the stable branch key. A bare built-in leaking off a public entry point breaks that contract silently. The budget gates the contract mechanically — the verb surface is held to zero off-contract raises and the rest is frozen — so a new bare-built-in raise must be converted to a VincioError or deliberately reviewed into the baseline, exactly as the surface gate freezes the two-level __all__.

slos.statement: No public method of the user-facing ContextApp facade (the app.* verb surface) raises a bare built-in exception; every raise there is a VincioError subclass.
slos.rationale: The app.* verbs are the canonical public entry point. This is the always-on half of the error-contract gate — it needs no allowlist and bites the moment a verb leaks a raw ValueError/KeyError/NotImplementedError, so the surface a caller wraps in `except VincioError` never surprises them with an off-contract exception.

slos.statement: The error-contract detector provably bites: an injected bare built-in raise on a public def is reported while an encapsulated private-def raise is not, so a green check means the invariant was actually exercised.
slos.rationale: A gate that never fires is indistinguishable from no gate. The budget gates that the static detector flags a public built-in raise and ignores an encapsulated one, so the error-contract guarantee is backed by a check that demonstrably catches the leak it claims to prevent without over-reporting.

slos.statement: No best-effort fallback swallows a broad exception silently: every broad except (Exception / BaseException / a bare except / contextlib.suppress(Exception)) on a public module re-raises, records its failure observably (a logger call or note_suppressed), or carries a justifying # noqa: BLE001 — and the detector provably catches an injected silent swallow while ignoring a logged one.
slos.rationale: A broad except that catches and continues is sound policy, but one that swallows the exception silently hides a real bug — a quietly degraded result with nothing to trace it to. The budget gates the contract mechanically: the lint holds the whole public tree to zero unmarked silent swallows, so a new silent swallow fails the build unless it is made observable (note_suppressed logs it on the vincio.suppressed channel and counts it) or deliberately marked, exactly as the surface gate freezes the two-level __all__ and the error-contract gate holds the verb surface to zero off-contract raises.

slos.statement: Every public module is free of unmarked silent broad-except swallows: the lint reports none tree-wide.
slos.rationale: A silent broad swallow reads as a sound fallback while hiding the failure it catches. The budget gates that no public module carries one, so a best-effort fallback is always observable (logged and counted) or explicitly justified with # noqa: BLE001 — the debt cannot silently return.

slos.statement: The observable-failure detector provably bites: an injected silent broad swallow is reported while a logged one is not, so a green check means the invariant was actually exercised.
slos.rationale: A gate that never fires is indistinguishable from no gate. The budget gates that the static detector flags a silent broad except and ignores a logged one, so the observable-failure guarantee is backed by a check that demonstrably catches the silent swallow it claims to prevent without over-reporting.

slos.statement: Every public capability is reachable through a production path: each entry in the wire-or-retire ledger resolves to a live reach (an app.* verb, an engine method, a registration helper, or a public class member) and — for a wired one — is referenced by production code outside its defining module, and the detector provably bites on an unreachable reach and a wired symbol with no production caller.
slos.rationale: A capability that is public but that nothing can reach — no app.* verb, no example, no internal caller — reads as supported API while being dead. The budget gates the contract mechanically: the guard holds a frozen ledger of the formerly-unhooked capabilities and fails the build if one loses its production entry point, exactly as the surface gate freezes the two-level __all__ and the error-contract gate holds the verb surface to zero off-contract raises — so a capability stays wired (an app.* verb / an internal caller) or is retired from the surface, never left dead.

slos.statement: Every capability in the wire-or-retire ledger is reachable: the guard reports no unreachable reach and no wired symbol that has become dead surface.
slos.rationale: An unhooked public symbol reads as supported API while being unreachable. The budget gates that every listed capability keeps a production entry point, so the wire-or-retire debt cannot silently return.

slos.statement: The wire-or-retire detector provably bites: an injected check whose reach does not resolve, and an injected wired symbol with no production caller, are each reported — so a green check means the invariant was actually exercised.
slos.rationale: A gate that never fires is indistinguishable from no gate. The budget gates that the guard flags an unreachable reach and a dead wired symbol, so the wire-or-retire guarantee is backed by a check that demonstrably catches the unhooked capability it claims to prevent.

slos.statement: Every docstring that advertises a behaviour either performs it or is corrected: the budget allocator exposes no reclaim it does not run, the compression tuner gates on the faithfulness metric its docstring names, the federated default-deny consent path refuses deterministically, and delete/forget share one body with audit semantics preserved — each re-derived from the live code.
slos.rationale: A docstring that promises behaviour the code no longer performs is a quiet lie that misleads a reader who trusts it. The budget re-derives each reconciled claim from the live code, so a docstring and its behaviour cannot silently diverge again — the parity guarantee is backed by a check that exercises the very behaviour each docstring describes.

slos.statement: The token-budget allocator advertises no separate redistribute reclaim: the method is gone and the allocator hands every non-fixed token to the flexible blocks at allocation time, exactly as the module docstring describes.
slos.rationale: The budgeting module docstring once promised a reclaim step that nothing invoked. The budget gates that the dead method stays gone and the described allocation actually distributes the whole remainder, so the claim cannot drift back into a promise the code does not keep.

slos.statement: The learned-compression docstring matches the gate: CompressionTuner reads the faithfulness eval metric it names, and compression_faithfulness / faithfulness_preserved are the offline fidelity measures, preserving answer-bearing units.
slos.rationale: The llmlingua docstring claimed the tuner called the faithfulness helpers directly; it gates on a faithfulness eval metric instead. The budget gates that the tuner reads that metric and the helpers measure fidelity as described, so the documented adoption gate is the one the code runs.

slos.statement: The federated default-deny consent demonstration is deterministic: a store-less default-deny consent ledger refuses an ungranted subject and a grant flips it, regardless of any consent persisted to disk from an earlier run.
slos.rationale: The example's default-deny refusal only fired against a pristine store, so a second run that loaded a persisted grant silently stopped refusing. The budget gates the enforcement semantics the example demonstrates — refuse without a grant, allow with one — so the guarantee fires every run, not only the first.

slos.statement: MemoryEngine.delete delegates to forget — one body — with the audit semantics preserved: a plain delete records no reason, forget records one.
slos.rationale: delete and forget were near-duplicate bodies that could drift apart on edit. The budget gates that delete delegates to forget and the two audit details stay distinct, so the deletion path has one implementation and one place a bug can hide.

slos.statement: The library is robust under python -O: no public module carries an assert that carries control-flow weight and would silently vanish when assertions are stripped — every load-bearing assert is an explicit guard that raises a VincioError, and a genuine never-happens invariant kept as an assert carries a justifying # noqa: S101 — and the detector provably flags an injected bare assert while ignoring a marked one.
slos.rationale: python -O (and -OO) strips every assert, so a load-bearing one — narrowing a value the code then dereferences, or checking a precondition a public operation depends on — vanishes in an optimized deployment, turning a caught invariant into an opaque downstream TypeError far from its cause. The budget gates the contract mechanically: each load-bearing assert is replaced by a guard that raises the appropriate VincioError so an optimized run fails loudly and correctly, and the lint holds the whole public tree to zero unmarked asserts, so a new one fails the build unless it is converted to a guard or deliberately marked — exactly as the observable-failure gate holds the tree to zero unmarked silent swallows and the error-contract gate holds the verb surface to zero off-contract raises.

slos.statement: Every public module is free of unmarked asserts: the lint reports none tree-wide, so nothing load-bearing is left to vanish under python -O.
slos.rationale: An assert stripped under -O reads as a sound check while doing nothing in an optimized deployment. The budget gates that no public module carries an unmarked one, so a control-flow-significant invariant is always an explicit guard that raises a VincioError or an explicitly justified # noqa: S101 — the debt cannot silently return.

slos.statement: The -O-robustness detector provably bites: an injected bare assert is reported while a marked one is not, so a green check means the invariant was actually exercised.
slos.rationale: A gate that never fires is indistinguishable from no gate. The budget gates that the static detector flags an unmarked assert and ignores a # noqa: S101-marked one, so the -O-robustness guarantee is backed by a check that demonstrably catches the stripped-under-O assert it claims to prevent without over-reporting.

slos.statement: No dead-but-resolvable public surface: every public symbol (top-level vincio.__all__ plus every public subpackage __all__) is either used somewhere in the code corpus (vincio + tests + examples + benchmarks) or declared, with a structural reason, in the frozen reachability baseline (docs/reference/reachability.txt) — the baseline is exactly the live unreferenced set, and the detector provably flags an injected dead symbol and a stale baseline entry.
slos.rationale: The surface gate proves every public name resolves; it cannot tell a live, used symbol from one that resolves yet is referenced nowhere — the dead-but-resolvable surface the 6.0 phase removed by a one-time manual reference check that was never mechanized, so the exact debt could silently return. The budget completes the audit and locks it in: the reference check is run over the whole surface on every build, a pure helper is exercised by a test (so it drops out) and a structurally-unexercisable symbol (an abstract base a user implements, an optional-dependency provider/backend, a socket/Redis/webhook-binding sink) is a reviewed baseline entry, so a new dead public symbol fails the build the moment it lands — exactly as the surface gate freezes the two-level __all__ and registry coverage keeps the model catalog honest.

slos.statement: No public symbol is referenced nowhere in the code corpus and left undeclared: every unreferenced public symbol is classified in the reachability baseline, so there is no undeclared dead public surface tree-wide.
slos.rationale: A public symbol that resolves but is used nowhere reads as supported API while doing nothing. The budget gates that every such symbol is either exercised by a test or a reviewed BASE/OPTDEP/WIRING baseline entry, so a new dead-but-resolvable symbol cannot ship unnoticed — the always-on, no-allowlist-bypass half of the reachability gate.

slos.statement: The reachability baseline is exactly the live unreferenced public set — no missing entry (an undeclared dead symbol) and no stale one (a baselined symbol that has since gained a reference) — and the rendered manifest matches docs/reference/reachability.txt byte-for-byte.
slos.rationale: A baseline that drifts from reality is a stale allowlist that hides the very debt it was meant to track. The budget keeps it minimal and honest: a symbol that becomes referenced must be removed from the baseline, and any edit is a reviewed diff against the committed manifest, the way subpackage-surface.txt freezes the two-level surface.

slos.statement: The reachability detector provably bites: an injected unreferenced symbol absent from the baseline is reported, a baselined symbol that is actually referenced is flagged stale, and a referenced symbol is not reported — so a green check means the invariant was actually exercised.
slos.rationale: A gate that never fires is indistinguishable from no gate. The budget gates that the reference scan flags a dead symbol and a stale baseline entry while crediting a real load (including an aliased import, the case that once made a used symbol look dead), so the no-dead-surface guarantee is backed by a check that demonstrably catches the failure it claims to prevent without over-reporting.
